issue: getAllTradeRecords loads the user's ENTIRE TradeTable with no LIMIT/pagination, then maps every row into a new object and reverses the whole array in memory. It is invoked from apps/app/src/app/private/layout.tsx:15 on every navigation under /private/*, so the full result set is materialized and serialized into the RSC payload on each page hit.
evidence: const data = await db.query.TradeTable.findMany({ where: eq(TradeTable.userId, userId) }); const processedData = data.map(...); return [...processedData].reverse();
estimated_impact: Grows unbounded with trade count. At ~1KB/trade and a heavy user with 10k trades that is ~10MB read from PG + a second ~10MB copy (map) + a third (spread+reverse) per request, plus full JSON serialization into the RSC stream. Easily +300-800ms p95 and ~30MB transient heap per /private/* render for power users; no cache so it repeats on every navigation.
recommendation: Add server-side pagination/LIMIT + ORDER BY createdAt DESC (push the reverse into the DB query using the existing userIdCloseDateIndex or a createdAt index) and only fetch the columns the layout needs. Avoid the spread copy: reverse in the query, not in JS. Consider caching per user via unstable_cache/React cache() so navigation between /private pages reuses the result.

issue: The private layout runs three independent DB round-trips serially on every render: auth(), getAllTradeRecords() (1 query), then getAllStrategies() which itself does a UserTable existence findFirst (extra query) AND a second auth() call before selecting strategies. These are awaited one after another instead of in parallel.
evidence:
const tradeRecords = await getAllTradeRecords();  // awaits fully
... const strategiesResult = await getAllStrategies(userId);  // then awaits fully; internally re-calls auth() + UserTable.findFirst
estimated_impact: 4 sequential round-trips (auth + trades + user-exists + strategies) on the critical render path of every /private page. At ~5-15ms/query that is +20-60ms p50 that could be ~half if parallelized; the redundant UserTable.findFirst inside getAllStrategies and the duplicate auth() call are pure waste.
recommendation: Run the trades and strategies fetches with Promise.all. Drop the redundant UserTable existence check from the strategy read path (it is a read, not a write) or fold it into a single JOIN. Pass the already-resolved userId into getAllStrategies instead of having it call auth() again.

issue: getReports filters ReportsTable by userId and ORDER BY createdAt DESC, but ReportsTable (schema.ts:101) has NO index on user_id (or user_id, created_at). TransactionsTable (schema.ts:109) is the same. Trade/Strategy/Journal tables all have userId indexes; Reports/Transactions were missed.
evidence: schema.ts:101 ReportsTable has only id PK, no index() on user_id; getReports: .where(eq(ReportsTable.userId, userId)).orderBy(desc(ReportsTable.createdAt))
estimated_impact: Sequential scan + in-memory sort of the whole reports table on every reports-history page load. With 100k rows across all users that is roughly +200ms-1s per query and scales with TOTAL table size, not per-user rows. Also pulls full reportData JSONB via select() (see separate finding).
recommendation: Add index("reports_user_id_created_at_idx").on(table.userId, table.createdAt) (and a user_id index on TransactionsTable), then run drizzle-kit generate per CLAUDE.md. This turns the scan+sort into an index range scan.

issue: getReports uses db.select() (SELECT *) which pulls the full reportData JSONB blob for every report, even though the function only needs id, createdAt, isFavorite and three array lengths from reportData. The large AI report JSON is transferred from PG and parsed just to compute numberOfMessages.
evidence: const reports = await db.select().from(ReportsTable).where(...).orderBy(...); ... numberOfMessages: report.reportData.instruments.length + report.reportData.moneyManagement.length + ...
estimated_impact: Each AI report JSON can be several KB-tens of KB. Fetching N full blobs to read three lengths wastes PG I/O and Node JSON parse time; with 200 reports of ~20KB that is ~4MB transferred + parsed per list load (qualitative: +50-200ms and extra GC pressure).
recommendation: Either store/maintain a numberOfMessages (or counts) column updated on save, or compute the lengths via a SQL expression (jsonb_array_length) and select only the needed scalar columns. At minimum, select explicit columns instead of SELECT *.

issue: Every GET/PATCH reads features.json from disk with fs.readFile and JSON.parse on each request (route is force-dynamic). Both MCP servers (stdio + HTTP wrapper) and the admin server action call this endpoint, and the stdio MCP refetches ALL flags before every single mutation (fetchFlags() then patchFlag()).
evidence: route.ts:32 const fileContent = await fs.readFile(getFilePath(), 'utf-8'); return NextResponse.json(JSON.parse(fileContent)); — plus mcps/feature-flags/src/index.ts:197 fetchFlags() immediately before every patchFlag().
estimated_impact: features.json is ~4.8KB today so disk+parse is ~0.5-1ms, but it is on the feature-flags hot path and every MCP set_feature_state/adjust_traffic does 2 HTTP+file round-trips (read-all then read-all-again inside PATCH). Qualitative now; becomes a real bottleneck if flag evaluation moves onto request paths or the file grows.
recommendation: Cache parsed flags in module scope with an mtime/`fs.watch` invalidation, or move flags to a small KV/table. In the MCP, the GET+PATCH is inherently racy and double-reads; have PATCH return the full updated set so the client need not refetch.

issue: The paid Claude endpoint reads request.json() with no body-size cap and JSON.stringifies the entire client-supplied trades array straight into the LLM prompt. A large trades payload is parsed, re-stringified, and sent to Anthropic with no length/size guard. follow-up-claude/route.ts has the same pattern plus string concatenation of trades+prevResponse.
evidence: claude/route.ts:10 const data = await request.json(); ... text: JSON.stringify(trades) — no size/length validation before the model call.
estimated_impact: Unbounded JSON parse + stringify on the event loop (each MB of trades ~ a few ms of sync blocking) and unbounded upstream token cost/latency. A malicious or careless client can send a multi-MB array, blocking the loop and amplifying Anthropic latency/cost. Cross-ref: security-review.md flags this as a cost-amplification DoS / no body-size cap.
recommendation: Validate and cap the trades payload (max records + max bytes) before stringifying; reject oversized bodies with 413. Stream or truncate to a bounded window of recent trades for the analysis.

issue: Token decrement is a read-modify-write across two awaited steps (findFirst then update with the computed value) that also runs serially before the long Anthropic call. Not only a correctness race (security-review.md MEDIUM 'non-atomic token decrement') but it adds an extra serial DB round-trip on the request before the LLM call and is not done atomically in SQL.
evidence: const user = await db.query.UserTable.findFirst(...); ... const updatedTokens = user.tokens - 1; await db.update(UserTable).set({ tokens: updatedTokens })...
estimated_impact: Extra serial round-trip (~5-15ms) plus a guaranteed lost-update under concurrent requests (two parallel calls both read N, both write N-1, user is charged once for two analyses). The Anthropic call (multi-second) then runs while the row is in this stale state.
recommendation: Decrement atomically in one statement with a guard: UPDATE user SET tokens = tokens - 1 WHERE id = ? AND tokens > 0 RETURNING tokens; treat zero rows affected as insufficient tokens. Removes both the race and the extra round-trip.

issue: Ingestion accumulates every chunk's full embedding vector (1024 or 1536 floats each) into an in-memory points[] array for the entire corpus before upserting, and generates embeddings strictly one-by-one in a sequential await loop (no concurrency/batching of the embedding step).
evidence: const allChunks = []; for (...) allChunks.push(...docChunks); ... const points = []; for (let i ...) { const vector = await getEmbedding(chunk.pageContent); points.push(record); }
estimated_impact: Memory: ~1024 floats * 8B = ~8KB/vector held for the whole run; 10k chunks ~80MB+ of vectors retained until the end. Throughput: embeddings are fully serial, so a corpus of N chunks takes N * per-embedding latency with zero overlap. Build-time script, so impact is bounded, but a large corpus will OOM / run very slowly.
recommendation: Stream: embed and upsert in fixed-size batches (e.g. 100) and let points be GC'd after each upsert instead of holding the whole corpus. Optionally embed with bounded concurrency (p-limit) to overlap model calls. (Build-time, so MEDIUM not HIGH.)

issue: Each mutating MCP tool call (set_feature_state, adjust_traffic_rollout) performs fetchFlags() to GET the entire flag set, then patchFlag() which on the server side re-reads the whole file again. So one logical mutation = 2 full reads + 1 write of the flags file. The HTTP wrapper (http.ts) repeats the same fetch-then-patch shape.
evidence: const flags = await fetchFlags(); validateStateChange(flags, ...); const updatedFlag = await patchFlag(...); — and PATCH in route.ts:52 reads the file again.
estimated_impact: O(2) redundant full reads per mutation. Small absolute cost today (~4.8KB file) but it is a read-then-write with no locking → also a lost-update race when two mutations interleave. Qualitative latency; correctness risk under concurrency.
recommendation: Have the validate step reuse the flags the PATCH endpoint already loads (do validation server-side and skip the client GET), or pass an optimistic version/ETag so PATCH can reject stale writes. Reduces to one read + one guarded write.

issue: Inside the per-chunk loop, fs.appendFile is awaited for every single chunk (one syscall per chunk) to build chunks.jsonl, serializing disk writes with the embedding generation.
evidence: for (let i = 0; i < allChunks.length; i++) { ... await fs.appendFile(CHUNKS_FILE, JSON.stringify({...}) + '\n'); }
estimated_impact: One open/write/close-style append syscall per chunk; for 10k chunks that is 10k awaited disk ops interleaved with embedding. Build-time only, so LOW, but it needlessly serializes I/O. ~tens of ms to seconds aggregate on large corpora.
recommendation: Accumulate JSONL lines in a buffer and write once at the end, or use a single createWriteStream and write() to it, instead of an awaited appendFile per iteration.

issue: Read-path actions getAllStrategies (and the write paths saveStrategy/editStrategy) each do a separate UserTable.findFirst 'profile exists' check before the real query. On the read path this is a pure extra round-trip that gates a SELECT.
evidence: getAllStrategies: const user = await db.query.UserTable.findFirst({ where: eq(UserTable.id, userId) }); ... then the strategies SELECT.
estimated_impact: +1 serial DB round-trip (~5-15ms) on every strategies read, which runs in the /private layout on every navigation (compounds with the layout finding). Negligible per call but on a hot path it adds up.
recommendation: On the read path, skip the existence check (an empty strategies array is a fine response for a profileless user), or combine into a single query/JOIN. Keep the check only where a real FK/UX gate is needed.

issue: Both claude routes log the full raw LLM response and prompt material to stdout (console.log of raw Claude response, returning the entire SDK res object). On large analyses this serializes big strings to the log transport on every paid call.
evidence: claude/route.ts:75 console.log('Raw Claude response:', responseText); line 85 console.log('Cleaned Claude response:', responseText); follow-up returns JSON.stringify(res) (whole SDK object).
estimated_impact: Logging multi-KB strings per request adds sync stringify + log-transport cost on the hot paid path and bloats logs; qualitative (a few ms + log volume). Also overlaps a security finding (raw LLM output / verbose logging).
recommendation: Gate verbose response logging behind a debug flag and log only lengths/metadata in production; return only the needed fields rather than the entire SDK response object.
